title: The interaction of the zone of calcified cartilage and subchondral bone in osteoarthritis
abstract:
The Interaction of the Zone of Calciﬁed Cartilage
and Subchondral Bone in Osteoarthritis
THEODORE R. OEGEMA, JR.,1,2* RANDALL J. CARPENTER,1 FRANCINE HOFMEISTER,1
AND ROBY C. THOMPSON, JR.1
1Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, University of Minnesota, Minneapolis, Minnesota
2Department of Biochemistry, University of Minnesota, Minneapolis, Minnesota
KEY WORDS
vascular invasion; osteoarthritis; cartilage; calciﬁcation
ABSTRACT
The zone of calciﬁed cartilage (ZCC) forms an important interface between
cartilage and bone for transmitting force, attaching cartilage to bone, and limiting diffusion from
bone to the deeper layers of cartilage. The height of the ZCC is a relatively constant percent of
articular cartilage and the height is maintained by a balance between progression of the tidemark
into the unmineralized cartilage and changing into bone by vascular invasion and bony remodeling.
During its formation, the cells that form the ZCC have properties similar to the cells of the growth
plate. In the adult, the ZCC becomes quiescent but not inactive. The ZCC may be reactivated in
osteoarthritis and may progressively calcify the unmineralized cartilage. This might contribute to
cartilage thinning which would increase the concentration of forces across the uncalciﬁed cartilage
leading to more damage.
Although the subchondral bony plate remodels extensively in osteoarthritis, there is little
evidence that a change in the biomechanics of the plate directly initiates the osteoarthritic process in
cartilage. However, increased repair by endochondral ossiﬁcation of vertical cracks in the ZCC that
penetrate into the marrow space could contribute to progression via changes in the ZCC. Microsc.
Res. Tech. 37:324–332, 1997.
r 1997 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
INTRODUCTION
The zone of calciﬁed cartilage (ZCC) is the highly
modiﬁed mineralized region of articular cartilage that
provides the attachment of cartilage to bone, provides
for the transition of force transmission across the joint,
and limits diffusion from subchondral bone (Oegema
and Thompson, 1990a). It is a region that changes
dramatically in osteoarthritis so much so that one of the
hallmarks of osteoarthritis has been the duplication of
the ‘‘tidemark’’ which is a gently wavy line at the
junction between the non-mineralized and mineralized
cartilage interface. Its continued activity as a growth
plate in osteoarthritis has long been the subject of much
speculation (Bullough et al., 1985; Hulth, 1993; Johnson,
1958; Ogston, 1878). With the exception of a few
studies, almost all our knowledge about the formation,
development, maturation, aging, and possible changes
in osteoarthritis of the ZCC have come from the applica-
tion of microscopic techniques. In this present study,
the ZCC and its relationship to the subchondral bone
will be examined from the perspective of the micro-
scopic methods used to approach the problem.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Animal Model
Experimental groups consisted of groups of nine or
more male New Zealand White rabbits with known
birth dates from 4 months to 7 months (Birchwood
Farms, Redwing, MN). All rabbits received into each
knee an interarticular injection of 9.25 mg/kg of 2,4-bis
(N,N8-dicarboxymethylaminomethyl ﬂuorescein (DCAF,
United States Biochemical Corp., Cleveland, OH), dis-
solved in 2% NaHCO3 (Oegema and Thompson, 1990b),
and in the older groups (6- and 7-month groups), 20
mg/kg oral disodium etidronate (Norwich Eaton Phar-
maceuticals, Inc., Norwich, NY) was given for 2 days
following DCAF treatment (Oegema et al., 1996). At the
time of sacriﬁce, animals were injected intramuscularly
with Ketamine HCl (36 mg/kg; Ketaset, Aveco, Inc.,
Fort Dodge, IA) combined with Xylazine (8 mg/kg;
AnaSed, Lloyd Laboratories, Shenandoah, IA), followed
in 10 minutes by an intracardial injection of Beuthana-
sia-D Special (1 ml/10 LB; Schering-Plough, Animal
Health Corp., Kenilworth, NJ by Steris Laboratories,
Phoenix, AZ) and desired tissue resected. In order to get
a measurable rate of migration, the timing for labeling
of the experimental groups was determined from pilot
projects. Rabbits from 4 months were sacriﬁced 2 weeks
after injection and the 7-month-old animals at up to 7
weeks after injection.
Histological Studies
The patellae from rabbits were ﬁxed in absolute
ethanol at room temperature and then embedded in
methyl methacrylate and sectioned with a diamond saw
at 100 µm as previously described for canine patellae
(Oegema and Thompson, 1990b). In order to follow
movement of the zone of calciﬁed cartilage, the sections
were viewed under a Zeiss ﬂuorescent microscope with
the appropriate ﬂuorescein ﬁlters. The samples were
*Correspondence to: Dr. T.R. Oegema, Dept. of Orthopaedic Surgery, Box 310
UMHC, 420 Delaware St., SE, Minneapolis, MN 55455.
Received 11 October 1994; Accepted 21 November 1994
MICROSCOPY RESEARCH AND TECHNIQUE 37:324–332 (1997)
r 1997 WILEY-LISS, INC.
 backlit with transmitted light so that the tidemark
could be seen and measurements could be made with a
calibrated reticle. Movement from the calciﬁed inter-
face was monitored by observing the middle of the
ﬂuorescent band and four to ﬁve measurements were
made on each of three sections. The average values of
these measurements were used to compare samples.
In order to determine cell viability, mature (2–4-year-
old) male canine femoral groove cartilages were har-
vested under sterile conditions and incubated for 18
hours at 37°C in 95% air, 5% CO2 in RPMI-1640
medium containing 0.5 mg/ml of nitroblue tetrazolium.
After incubation, the samples were ﬁxed in 10% phos-
phate-buffered formaldehyde, pH 7.4, decalciﬁed as
above, frozen-sectioned at 7 µ, and stained with Safra-
nin O, fast green (Donohue et al., 1983).
Human trapeziometacarpal joints, recovered at time
of surgery for regular light level histology, were ﬁxed in
10% formalin, decalciﬁed in 10% formic acid and 10%
formaldehyde, embedded in paraffin, sectioned at 7 µ,
and stained with Safranin O, fast green (Donohue et al.,
1983). Samples were viewed with a Zeiss microscope
equipped with a polarizer attachment. For photo-
graphs, the polarizers were crossed to obtain maximal
darkness. The sample was rotated until optimum sam-
ple polarization was seen and then the polarizer un-
crossed until the nonpolarized regions could be seen
and photographed.
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
Anatomy of the Zone of Calciﬁed Cartilage
Descriptions of the mature ZCC have mainly been
derived from light-level microscopic studies on decalci-
ﬁed paraffin-embedded sections (Fig. 1). The ZCC is
bounded on one side by the subchondral bone. This
interface is highly irregular and transmission electron
microscopic studies have shown that no ﬁbers cross the
interface (Clark and Huber, 1990 and references
therein). This means the two tissues are held together
only by interdigitation, the extent of which may be
controlled by the rates of vascular invasion and bone
remodeling. The other interface is the tidemark and is
the border between mineralized and non-mineralized
cartilage. The tidemark has gentle waviness. Some
increase in waviness of the tidemark is shown in the
knee depending on whether it is covered or uncovered
by the meniscus (Bullough et al., 1985). If unmineral-
ized cartilage is removed with proteases, such as pa-
pain, and the exposed surface viewed in the scanning
electron microscope the surface is relatively smooth
with small pits representing cells at the surface and
occasional large holes representing blood vessels that
touch surface (Bullough and Jagannath, 1983; Clark
and Huber, 1990; Thompson et al., 1993).
The tidemark will stain with dyes that bind to
protein and lipid but not proteoglycan (Bullough and
Jagannath, 1983). Although the results of an early
transmission electron microscopy study (Redler et al.,
1975) suggested there might be speciﬁc proteins at the
tidemark that could serve the biomechanical function of
tethering the collagen ﬁbrils, the general consensus is
that the tidemark is the result of accumulation of
nonspeciﬁc molecules at the interface caused by discon-
tinuous mineralization. Multiple tidemarks may be
readily identiﬁed in normal joints of all species (Fig. 1)
and are not a unique feature of osteoarthritis. The
existence of multiple tidemarks means mineralization
may go in ‘‘starts and stops.’’ The closest distance
between tidemarks may be about the height of a cell
(Fig. 1). The biological and biomechanical controls for
this process are not understood.
The thickness of the ZCC has been measured in
different species (Donohue et al., 1983; Kiviranta et al.,
1987) and in different joints in humans (Mu¨ller-Gerbl,
1987; Oettmeier et al., 1989). The thickness is a rela-
tively constant percent of the total articular cartilage at
approximately 5% with a range of 3 to 8%. Because of
the irregular nature of the ZCC, it is difficult to get an
average thickness. So changes are frequently docu-
mented by measuring the area of the ZCC and dividing
by the linear distance along the base of the tidemark
(Donohue et al., 1983; Mu¨ller-Gerbl, 1987). Since the
total thickness of cartilage is related to the forces across
the joint, this implies that mechanical forces play a role
in determining the ZCC thickness. This hypothesis has
been directly tested only once where after long-term,
long-distance running (40 km/day) the total cartilage
thickness and thus the ZCC of the knees of young
growing beagles was shown to increase with running
(Kiviranta et al., 1988; Oettmeier et al., 1992). Interest-
ingly, the thickness of the ZCC is also increased with
immobilization and returns to normal after remobiliza-
tion suggesting tidemark advancement is coupled to
resorption (Kiviranta et al., 1987).
Biomechanics of the Zone of Calciﬁed Cartilage
Biomechanically, besides attaching bone to cartilage,
the ZCC diffuses transfer forces across the bone-
cartilage interface. With the gentle undulation of the
tidemark and the perpendicular orientation of the
ﬁbrils, forces that cross the tidemark would dissipate
forces at this transition (Redler et al., 1975). It may also
relieve force gradients between bone and cartilage. The
ZCC although calciﬁed to the same extent as bone is
only about 1⁄10 as stiff, perhaps because of the parallel
alignment of the collagen ﬁbrils (Mente and Lewis,
1994). The consequence of this may be attenuation of
the force gradient across the joint (Mu¨ller-Gerbl et al.,
1987). However, although less stiff than bone, the ZCC
is still 10–100-fold stiffer than cartilage so it still may
be too stiff to be effective (Mente and Lewis, 1994). In
addition, the ZCC forms an effective barrier to diffusion
from the underlying bone and limiting nutrition to the
deeper layers of cartilage (Clark and Huber, 1990).
Mineralization of the Zone of Calciﬁed Cartilage
The biochemical composition data for young mature
bovine steer support the mineral’s being calcium hy-
droxyapatite with a mineral content of 65% and miner-
alization is accompanied by a 50% decrease in proteogly-
can content (Lovell and Eyre, 1988). Green et al. (1970)
found by x-ray methods that the degree of mineraliza-
tion did not vary with age but did vary with species.
Brown et al. (1993) investigated the differences be-
tween the mineral deposition pattern of the growth
plate and the ZCC by removing the unmineralized
phase of both tissues and examining the shape of the
mineralized phase. In the growth plate the cells, espe-
cially at the time of mineralization, make up a much
325
